does: This paper introduces Reference-Aligned UnLearning (RAUL), a multi-objective framework for machine unlearning that removes the influence of specified training samples while maintaining overall model performance. Rather than using unbounded loss maximization for forgetting, RAUL replaces it with a bounded KL divergence objective that aligns predictions on forgotten samples toward a reference distribution—either uniform or drawn from a held-out set—representing unseen data. The framework jointly optimizes two conflicting objectives: making the model forget target samples while retaining utility on remaining data. RAUL solves this multi-objective optimization problem using Jacobian descent, which aggregates conflicting gradients into a unified descent direction that avoids the catastrophic forgetting observed in single-objective approaches like gradient ascent or random relabeling.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature in machine unlearning, which addresses how to efficiently remove training data influence without full model retraining. The paper responds to limitations in existing gradient ascent and random relabeling methods that cause severe performance degradation due to unbounded forgetting objectives and unmanaged gradient conflicts between retention and forgetting goals. It connects to broader work on multi-objective optimization in machine learning and draws on reference-based alignment techniques from domain adaptation and transfer learning. The approach also relates to recent work on understanding and mitigating catastrophic forgetting in continual learning, since unlearning presents analogous challenges in maintaining previously learned knowledge while adapting model behavior.
enables: RAUL opens directions for more efficient unlearning procedures that could be incorporated into model governance and privacy-compliant machine learning pipelines. The bounded alignment approach suggests ways to formalize unlearning as constrained optimization rather than adversarial objectives, potentially leading to certified unlearning guarantees. The multi-objective optimization perspective enables future work exploring Pareto-optimal solutions for forgetting-retention trade-offs and principled methods for selecting reference distributions. This framework could facilitate practical deployment of right-to-be-forgotten mechanisms in production systems and inspire similar multi-objective approaches to other conflicting learning objectives in trustworthy AI.
